item.title: Manitoba government set to deliver budget amid health care, affordability concerns
item.description: Province has promised a balanced budget by 2027-28 but has so far missed its annual targets

item.title: How RCMP spies infiltrated the 1970s Indigenous rights movement
item.description: A CBC Indigenous investigation has found the Cold War-era RCMP Security Service had a widespread and intrusive surveillance operation targeting at least 30 legitimate Indigenous political organizations and hundreds of Indigenous people.

item.title: Smith-Carney pipeline deal to miss early deadlines, premier says
item.description: The first set of deadlines included in the energy and climate deal struck between Prime Minister Mark Carney and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith are not expected to be met, showing early challenges with the agreement that includes pursuing a new export pipeline from Alberta to the West Coast.

item.title: Sun, clouds and mild temperatures in Toronto today ahead of a week of mixed weather
Liberals to debate use of ‘nuclear option’ against notwithstanding clause
item.description: As the Supreme Court hears arguments on the notwithstanding clause's role in Canada, some Liberal party members are arguing for using the fede…

item.title: Burlington couple made ‘disgusting’ choices, but malnourished boy’s death not a murder, defence argues at close of trial
Future of corporate human-rights watchdog uncertain in Carney government
item.description: Ottawa won’t say whether budget cuts hit departments overseeing forced-labour laws

item.title: Ontario to seize ownership of Toronto Island Airport lands and declare it a special economic zone
item.description: Video

item.title: Nominee for Parliamentary Budget Officer says she will be ‘a one-term PBO’
item.description: Annette Ryan tells MPs she will provide high-quality, independent and relevant analysis

item.title: Police are banning pro-Palestine protesters from residential streets in this Toronto neighbourhood
Politics Insider: Supreme Court hears arguments on Quebec’s secularism law
item.description: The top court will examine in detail the Charter’s notwithstanding clause in relation to its use by Quebec to shield Bill 21

item.title: Doug Ford takes over Billy Bishop as Ottawa stays on sidelines amid Toronto pushback
item.description: On Monday, Ford announced Queen's Park was usurping the City of Toronto's place on the tripartite agreement that governs the island airfield.

item.title: Canada’s international student program blasted by auditor for failing to address ‘integrity concerns’
Canada’s immigration bill may compromise rights, UN committee says
Quebec’s secularism law threatens religious and equality rights, Supreme Court hears
The cost of this city project has more than tripled — and it’s now more than five years overdue. One Toronto councillor wants answers
item.description: Controversial drop-in centre for homeless women due to open in the former HQ for Craig and Marc Kielburger's charity, Free the Children.

item.title: What flags should fly at government buildings? Two Toronto councillors have clashing visions
item.description: Council will consider one request to ban all foreign flags at city hall and another to ceremonially fly the pre-revolutionary flag of Iran.

item.title: Peel police mourn death of beloved police dog Kylo: ‘His enthusiasm was constant’
‘Groundbreaking’ approach to youth mental health care in Toronto funded by $40-million donation
Carney did raise human rights with Xi, government clarifies, admitting earlier ‘error’
item.description: Public servants correct information government tabled suggesting PM did not ‘proactively’ raise human rights or foreign interference during Beijing visit

item.title: RCMP recruiting hurt by unrealistic targets, lengthy process, auditor general says
Costa Rica looks to Canada to safeguard rules-based trade, democracy in Latin America
Canada’s veterans rehab program under fire as MPs question contractor, federal officials
Canadian military members safe after reports of damage at UN peacekeeping camp
item.description: Canadian Armed Forces members deployed in the Middle East are safe after a missile was intercepted on Sunday near the Israel-Lebanon border, the defence minister's office says.

item.title: Liberals' fiscal watchdog nominee vows to hold government's 'feet to the fire'
item.description: The Liberal government's nominee to be the next fiscal watchdog insists she'll run an independent and non-partisan ship — even though her candidacy has created a political rift.

item.title: Ottawa’s foreign student cuts went far deeper than planned
item.description: The post Ottawa’s foreign student cuts went far deeper than planned appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: Saab signs deal with Canadian AI firm Cohere on Global Eye jets
Budget watchdog nominee promises ‘pointy analysis’ as MPs question independence
MPs debate contentious hate crimes legislation as House of Commons resumes
11 bomb threats at Toronto schools Monday but no credible threat, police say
Doug Ford says he would handily win re-election as he defends keeping cellphone records secret
Carney’s government admits ‘error,’ says PM did raise human rights with Xi
item.description: Carney met in January with Chinese President Xi Jinping and told reporters that he had spoken with Xi about human rights through direct conversations.

item.title: Toronto council asked to oppose Doug Ford’s push to bring jets to Billy Bishop airport: ‘It’s our waterfront’
What to expect when Doug Ford brings the legislature back on Monday
Doug Ford defends FOI changes, warns of enemies seeking government secrets
Immigration Department not keeping up with demand for student visa probes: auditor general
item.description: Auditor General Karen Hogan says the immigration department did not investigate or follow up on a large number of "high-risk cases" that weaken the integrity of the international student program.

item.title: RCMP failed to recruit enough officers to meet operational needs: auditor general
item.description: The RCMP has not recruited enough new officers or effectively assigned its members to meet its operational needs — raising concerns about public safety across the country, according to a new report from Canada's auditor general.

item.title: Auditor-General flags weaknesses in IRCC’s anti-fraud controls for international student program
item.description: Department failed to investigate more than 145,000 students flagged as not complying with the terms of their permits, report says

item.title: Auditor general flags potential risks in transition from Phoenix to Dayforce
item.description: The federal government is on pace to replace its troubled Phoenix pay system with a new platform, but risks including a backlog and rising costs remain, according to a report tabled Monday by Auditor General Karen Hogan.

item.title: Replacing Phoenix pay system will cost at least $4.2-billion, Auditor-General report says
item.description: Report finds federal efforts to eliminate backlog of pay complaints are falling short

item.title: What’s at stake in 3 upcoming byelections for the House of Commons
item.description: Three byelections are being held on April 13 and the results could have an impact on both the makeup of Parliament and how long it lasts.

item.title: Lawyers challenging Quebec's Bill 21 tell Supreme Court it violates Constitution, sets dangerous precedent
House of Commons returns as Iran war continues and audit reports due
item.description: Members of Parliament are back in the House of Commons today, where the government is expected to face tough questions about new auditor general reports and the war in Iran.

item.title: Opposition parties to pressure Ford government to reverse OSAP changes as legislature resumes
item.description: Opposition parties plan to lean on the Ford government to backtrack on contentious changes to student aid as MPPs return to Queen’s Park for the first time since December.

item.title: MPPs return to Queen’s Park today for the first time in 102 days. Here is what you can expect
item.description: MPPs are returning to the legislature at Queen’s Park this week for the first time since rising for a winter break from the chamber in December. Here’s some of what to expect in the next few weeks.

item.title: Canadians face ‘tsunami’ of transnational repression in coming years, cyber-research group says
item.description: Think tank cites rise of U.S. authoritarianism, AI and Carney’s disinterest in human rights

item.title: Confidential contract between feds, Stellantis reveals Brampton factory and worker terms
item.description: The multi-million-dollar deal at the heart of a high-profile fight between the Canadian government and one of the world’s biggest carmakers contains detailed clauses about the company’s footprint in Ontario, according to a copy obtained by CBC Windsor.

item.title: Dual citizens weigh Trump, taxes in decision to renounce U.S. citizenship
item.description: Every year, thousands of dual citizens living abroad choose to formally renounce their U.S. citizenship. They say Trump is a major factor in their decision — but not the only one.

item.title: Canada's border agency under fire for treatment of sexual harassment cases
item.description: The House of Commons public safety committee is planning an inquiry this spring to examine "systemic discrimination and organizational culture" within Canada's border agency.

item.title: Queen's Park resumes sitting after 14-week break, straight into budget season
item.description: Ontario’s legislature is set to resume sitting Monday after a 14-week break that ended in a veritable deluge of news, partial proposals and headline-grabbing musings from Premier Doug Ford and his government.

item.title: Toronto Region Board of Trade offers road map to tackle city’s traffic problem
Public servant ‘scared’ to take government’s retirement offer due to issues with Phoenix pay system
item.description: Jennifer MacDougall was told she owes the government about $10,500 because of a mistake in her pay file

item.title: Conservative campaign manager shrugs off polls showing Liberals have significant lead
item.description: The Conservative Party's campaign manager is shrugging off polls showing the Liberals hold a double-digit lead over the Tories — while Pierre Poilievre says he's broadening his image but won't change who he is.

item.title: Here are the five candidates competing to lead the federal NDP
What’s the future of virtual health care in Ontario? Despite funding cutback, it may have taken root
Canadians abroad call for increased voter turnout ahead of by-elections
item.description: Canadians living overseas argue barriers to casting a ballot could be affecting election results

item.title: MPs to question federal budget watchdog nominee as role remains unfilled
item.description: The Office of the Parliamentary Budget Officer scrutinizes federal spending decisions and helps to cost out campaign proposals during elections.

item.title: Elizabeth Renzetti: Doug Ford’s tried everything to keep his secrets. Changing the law is his last refuge
Doug Ford’s crackdown on ticket scalpers draws warnings it could push prices higher
item.description: Ontario's plan to crack down on what the premier calls resale "rip-offs" is raising questions about unintended consequences.

item.title: Canadian companies could face big losses as change looms in Cuba
item.description: Canada is the second-biggest source of foreign investment in Cuba, especially in tourism and mining. But Canadian companies with major operations in Cuba, including Sunwing and Sherritt International, now face unprecedented risks to their operations as the economy grinds to a halt and political change is in the air.

item.title: Federal departments and agencies to cut 12,000 full-time equivalent positions over three years
item.description: Expected cuts include 1,793 jobs at Public Services and Procurement Canada, 900 at Statistics Canada and 942 at Health Canada

item.title: Will Doug Ford’s ticket resale price cap put an end to $6,000 Taylor Swift seats? Experts are divided
item.description: Seven years after Ford removed a cap on resale prices — which his government called "unenforceable" — the premier now wants to make it illegal…

item.title: Toronto councillor violated code of conduct in heated video exchange with head of IntegrityTO, watchdog finds
ANALYSIS: Carney promised spending cuts — but not all cuts are equal
item.description: Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne says the government has found ways to cut spending by $60 billion but cautions that published spending cuts require important context.
